title: Premature greying of hair and beard
excerpt: Premature greying of hair and beard, known in Ayurveda as palitya, is not just cosmetic. The classical texts trace it to excess pitta and practical, cooling habits can genuinely slow it down.
body_html:
when the first grey shows up too early

You spot it in your late twenties, maybe younger: a silver thread at the temple, a streak in the beard, a handful of white hairs that were not there last season. You are not imagining it, and you are not alone. Premature greying is one of the most common concerns among men and women today, and Ayurveda has been observing and writing about it for well over two thousand years.

The classical term is palitya, which simply means the whitening or greying of hair before its natural time. The Sushruta Samhita lists palitya among conditions rooted in an aggravated state of pitta, the dosha associated with heat, metabolism, and transformation in the body (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana). When pitta rises beyond its normal measure, the texts say it can gradually damage the hair follicle and deplete the natural colour-sustaining nutrients that reach it.

The goal of rasayana in this context is to slow the process, nourish what remains, and prevent further aggravation, not to perform miracles.

what the granthas actually say

Pitta governs colour and lustre in Ayurvedic physiology. The Ashtanga Hridaya describes the hair as a by-product of asthi dhatu, the tissue layer associated with bones and hard structures, and notes that the nourishment of this tissue depends on a balanced flow of nutrients through the body (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sharirasthana). When pitta is aggravated or when the nutritional pipeline is disrupted, the tissues downstream, including hair, bear the cost.

The Bhavaprakasha, a later but widely respected compendium, discusses palitya in the context of rasayana, the branch of Ayurveda concerned with rejuvenation and longevity. It points to amla (Indian gooseberry, Emblica officinalis) as one of the most important substances for preserving hair colour and nourishing the scalp (Bhavaprakasha, Haritakyadi Varga). The same text notes that chronic stress, irregular sleep, and excessive sour, pungent, and fried foods all fan the pitta fire and can accelerate palitya.

The Sushruta Samhita also lists contributing factors that read like a description of modern urban life: suppression of natural urges, grief, irregular meals, and exposure to harsh sun and dust (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana). These are not just poetic observations. They describe real stressors on the body's nutritional and metabolic systems.

It is worth being honest about what the classical texts do not promise. Ayurveda frames palitya as manageable and, in some cases, partially reversible in its early stages through sustained lifestyle and diet changes. But it does not claim that every grey hair can be turned black again. The goal of rasayana in this context is to slow the process, nourish what remains, and prevent further aggravation, not to perform miracles.

daily habits that actually help

The most consistent recommendation across the granthas is head oiling. Warm sesame oil or coconut oil massaged gently into the scalp two to three times a week helps pacify pitta locally, improves circulation to the follicle, and keeps the scalp from drying out. If you can add a few drops of amla oil or use an amla-infused base, so much the better. Leave it on for at least thirty minutes before washing.

Diet matters more than most people expect. Cooling, sweet, and bitter foods are pitta-pacifying: ripe fruits, leafy greens, cucumber, coconut water, buttermilk, and whole grains. Amla is particularly valued, whether eaten fresh, dried, or as a churna (powder) mixed into warm water each morning. Reducing very spicy, sour, deep-fried, and fermented foods, along with cutting back on tea and coffee, gives the pitta fire less fuel.

Sleep and stress are the two factors people most often overlook. The texts are consistent on this point: chronic loss of sleep and unmanaged mental tension directly aggravate pitta. A wind-down routine, a fixed sleep time, and even ten minutes of pranayama (slow breathing exercises) in the morning can make a measurable difference over months, not days.

Protect the hair from direct harsh sun for long stretches, especially during summer afternoons. Covering the head with a light cotton cloth or cap is a simple, classical-era recommendation that still makes sense.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

Premature greying that appears very suddenly, spreads rapidly across the scalp, or is accompanied by patchy hair loss, changes in skin pigmentation, unusual fatigue, or unexplained weight changes should be evaluated by a doctor promptly. These can be signs of thyroid disorders, autoimmune conditions such as vitiligo or alopecia areata, or nutritional deficiencies including B12, iron, or copper, all of which need proper diagnosis and medical treatment.

A qualified vaidya can assess your prakriti (constitution) and the specific nature of your pitta aggravation before recommending herbs or formulations. Self-prescribing strong rasayana preparations without guidance is not advisable. This article is general information for healthy adults and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical or Ayurvedic care tailored to your individual situation.

Try This Today

  • Morning amla in warm water: Take half a teaspoon of amla churna (powder) in a glass of warm water each morning on an empty stomach. Give it at least two to three months of daily use before expecting visible change.
  • Warm oil scalp massage: Heat a small amount of sesame or coconut oil until just warm to the touch. Massage gently in circular motions over the scalp for five to ten minutes, two or three times a week. Leave on for thirty minutes then wash out.
  • Cut the pitta triggers: For one month, reduce very spicy, sour, and deep-fried food, along with black tea and coffee after noon. Note whether the scalp feels less inflamed or itchy, which is a sign pitta is settling.
  • Fixed sleep time: Sleep and wake at the same time every day, including weekends. Ten minutes of slow alternate-nostril breathing (anulom vilom) before bed supports the nervous system and reduces pitta-aggravating stress.
  • Cover the head in harsh sun: Wear a light cotton cap or drape a thin dupatta over the head during peak afternoon sun hours, especially between 11 am and 3 pm in summer.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Premature greying of hair and beard, known in Ayurveda as palitya, is not just cosmetic. The classical texts trace it to excess pitta and practical, cooling habits can genuinely slow it down.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Premature greying (palitya) is linked in classical texts to excess pitta and poor nourishment of hair tissue.
Amla, warm oil massage, and a cooling diet are the most consistently recommended Ayurvedic supports.
Sudden or rapid greying with other symptoms needs a doctor, not a home remedy.

glossary_terms.definition: The classical Ayurvedic term for premature or untimely greying of hair.
One of the three doshas, governing heat, metabolism, and transformation; its excess is the primary cause of palitya.
A branch of Ayurveda focused on rejuvenation, longevity, and the prevention of premature ageing.
The tissue layer associated with bones and hard structures; hair is considered a by-product of this layer.
A fine powder preparation of dried herbs, often taken with warm water.
An individual's inherent constitution, determined by the balance of doshas at birth.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, on palitya and pitta-related conditions (Nidanasthana and Uttaratantra)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sharirasthana, on hair as a by-product of asthi dhatu and its nourishment
Bhavaprakasha, Haritakyadi Varga (Nighantu), on amla (Emblica officinalis) and its role in rasayana for hair

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Palitya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Palitya is defined as: The classical Ayurvedic term for premature or untimely greying of hair.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to morning amla in warm water. Take half a teaspoon of amla churna (powder) in a glass of warm water each morning on an empty stomach. Give it at least two to three months of daily use before expecting visible change.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to warm oil scalp massage. Heat a small amount of sesame or coconut oil until just warm to the touch. Massage gently in circular motions over the scalp for five to ten minutes, two or three times a week. Leave on for thirty minutes then wash out.
